title: Spinning a four-ply yarn
subtitle: Plying four singles into a round, stable yarn for weaving and knitting
excerpt: A four-ply yarn is made by plying four singles together in two stages: first ply in pairs to make two two-ply yarns, then ply those two yarns together. The result is a round, strong yarn that resists pilling and suits weaving warps, hardwearing knitwear, and upholstery yarn.
sourceNotes: Four-ply construction synthesised from standard yarn-construction references. The technique is described in the Handweavers Guild of America yarn-construction guides and in general spinning manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: The first yarn produced directly from the drafted fibre on the wheel or spindle. Singles have an active twist that must be balanced by plying in the opposite direction.
A yarn made from two singles twisted together in the opposite direction to the singles twist. The first stage in four-ply construction.

The state when the singles twist and the plying twist are equal and opposite, so the finished yarn lies flat without kinking or spinning back on itself. A balanced yarn hangs straight when folded in half.
Wraps per inch: the number of times a yarn wraps around a ruler in one inch. Used to confirm yarn weight. A DK-weight four-ply runs around 11 to 14 wraps per inch when finished.

content.text: Four-ply construction requires four bobbins of
spun to a consistent weight, and two plying sessions. The first session makes two

yarns; the second session plies those together. Consistency in the singles is more important for four-ply than for simple two-ply, because any unevenness doubles in visibility through both plying stages. Spin all four bobbins in the same session from the same fibre source.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing away from the drive band and flyer. Tie hair back before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Prepared fibre (any breed), enough for four matched bobbins
Lazy kate or tensioned lazy kate

content.text: Spin four bobbins of consistent singles, all at the same twist angle. Divide the fibre evenly between the four bobbins before you start; weigh each portion so they are equal. Check the
periodically across all four bobbins to confirm consistency. Aim for the singles to be at least double the desired finished yarn weight; four-ply construction adds significant bulk.

Load two singles bobbins onto the lazy kate. Ply them together in the direction opposite to the singles twist (Z-spun singles are plied S). Use a slow treadle speed and allow the two threads to spiral around each other loosely before entering the orifice. Wind onto a clean bobbin. Repeat with the other two singles bobbins to make the second two-ply bobbin.

Load the two two-ply bobbins onto the lazy kate. Ply them together in the same direction as the first plying pass (both plying passes go in the same direction, opposite to the singles). This is the most common error in four-ply construction: many spinners mistakenly reverse direction for the second pass. Use a slow treadle speed and check
frequently by letting a loop of the yarn hang free: a balanced yarn hangs straight. Wind onto a clean bobbin, then wind off onto the niddy-noddy.

Soak the hank in lukewarm water for 20 minutes. Press out excess water in a towel without wringing. Hang with a light weight at the bottom to encourage the yarn to set straight. Four-ply yarn holds its balance well once twist-set and is noticeably more stable than two-ply for weaving warps.

items.problem: The finished yarn is unbalanced and kinks back on itself
items.cause: The second plying pass was made in the wrong direction (S instead of S, or Z instead of Z with the first pass)
items.fix: Confirm both plying passes travel in the same direction before the second pass. If the finished yarn is over-plied, add a mild weight to the hank and soak again; sometimes the fibres will relax slightly.

items.problem: The four-ply has an uneven texture, with thick and thin sections repeating
items.cause: The singles were not consistent across all four bobbins
items.fix: Spin more evenly next time; divide the fibre into four pre-weighed portions and check wpi at intervals. An Andean plying bracelet can help keep two bobbins at equal tension during the first plying pass.
